summary: Shapiro contrasts two kinds of engineering: theoretical calculation and hands-on physical intuition. He illustrates this with stories of bouncing on metal beams to test their strength for a motorized couch, and a classmate who tried to design a grass-cutting device using string on a hand-cranked pole without recognizing the obvious physical impracticality. The post argues that real engineering competence requires getting your hands dirty and testing materials physically, not just running calculations. He closes with a professor's wisdom that the best engineers learn by breaking things deliberately.
key_points: There are two kinds of engineering: desk-and-pencil calculation versus physical, hands-on testing — and competence requires both
Pure theoretical reasoning can lead you astray when you lack physical intuition about how materials and forces actually behave
The best way to understand a new material or technique is to beat on it, break it, and experiment with it directly
The best engineers aren't the ones who never fail — they're the ones who fail deliberately and learn from it
money_quotes: There are two kinds of engineering in this world. One kind takes place at a desk with a pencil and paper. The other takes place when you walk into Home Depot and jump on a wheelbarrow to determine if it can move the three-man rock in your yard without collapsing.
It seemed ballpeen-hammer-to-the-forehead-crease obvious to me that you couldn't cut grass by waving string at it, but not so Ziggy.

Ziggy graduated near the top of our class; he got top marks in almost all his engineering and math classes. But it would never occur to him to bounce on an iron shaft in a scrapyard as a basic design methodology.
Good electrical engineers don't blow any transistors in this lab. Bad ones blow four or five apiece. Really outstanding engineers destroy one, and they can tell you exactly how they did it.
Go blow a transistor. You'll thank me for it.
key_insight: Physical intuition and hands-on experimentation are as essential to good engineering as theoretical calculation — don't be the person who can solve the equations but can't tell that string on a hand crank won't cut grass.
